the closed physical analogy is:
If you had a policy on your physical lock system where
- locks are changed any time a key is used

With a cryptographic nonce
kafka_timestamp:
- there's only once copy of the current valid key exists per door
- a given key is only valid for a specific door (this is usually the case in cryptography - single
column7:
limited use)

With a "durqble nonce":
- all doors on the building could be opened with the same key
- there's a key issuer with an unlimited number of the current valid key
column8:

- any door can be the target

The latter is not necessarily insecure

offset: \"text\": \"selling nothing as something: satoshi\\u2019s bitcoin and the massive fantasy that followed when that anonymous individual using the alias satoshi nakamoto introduced bitcoin
kafka_timestamp: he claimed it was a payment system
column6: a means for enabling transactions. but a transaction assumes that something is being transferred. you cannot perform a payment if there is nothing to move from one party to another. there must always be an identifiable thing to begin with. yet this thing is missing from nakamoto\\u2019s creation. his system only maintains a decentralized list showing which numbers are assigned to which cryptographic keys. people began joining nakamoto\\u2019s creation by spending electricity to obtain these number assignments and paying to have them reassigned. in doing so
column7: they essentially gave up something in exchange for nothing. from that point onward
column8: a massive collective denial took hold as participants began to fantasize that they had actually received something. they often fantasize about digital objects. in reality
column12: the number \\u201c50\\u201d assigned to their cryptographic key cannot point to fifty distinct files
column14: or software artifacts. there are no digital objects to mine or buy. it is even more obvious that there is nothing physical. despite the common visual portrayal of bitcoin as metal coins stamped with symbols
column15: and despite frequent comparisons to collectibles or commodities
column16: no fifty tangible units of any kind are stored or reserved for the person whose key holds the number \\u201c50.\\u201d all such portrayals and comparisons are pure fantasy. however
column17: the most common fantasies among participants are that they hold something similar to fiat money
column18: e-money issued by companies like paypal
column20: or even stocks. yet in all those cases
column21: people hold records of legally binding obligations
column22: and they receive a return from the party that bears the obligation. that return can be direct or indirect. stocks represent a company\\u2019s obligation to its shareholders. when companies decide to distribute profits
column25: they are legally required to make direct payments to shareholders. paypal\\u2019s e-money and tokens like casino chips represent the issuer\\u2019s obligation to redeem them for a specified amount of fiat money. in other cases
column26: the return is indirect. fiat money is created through bank lending
column27: which means borrowers are legally obligated to repay banks. the only way they can fulfill that obligation is by producing goods
column29: or offering labor to those who hold fiat money. if the borrower is the government
column30: repayment occurs by enabling the settlement of tax liabilities with that money. if borrowers fail to meet their obligations
column31: banks seize their property and offer it at auction to holders of money. thus
column32: although holders have no direct claims against individual borrowers or banks
column37: and tax settlement from them precisely because they hold a record of existing legal obligations. in the bitcoin system
column38: no such record exist. there is no party that bears an obligation and that will provide a return
column40: to those who control the cryptographic keys. so

kafka_timestamp: pushing for blanket freezing or burning of \\\"vulnerable\\\" coins. be alert to the pattern. ### the psyop pattern watch for accounts that: - have lurked for years but suddenly surface with urgent demands - push blanket solutions (freeze all vulnerable coins!) without technical nuance - refuse to engage with existing solutions like quantumroot - rapidly escalate to painting opposition as crazy
column7: or \\\"in denial\\\" - frame theft as the \\\"only survivable solution\\\" the goal appears to be creating division and establishing precedent that developers can redistribute coins \\\"for the greater good.\\\" once that precedent exists
column8: the criteria can expand indefinitely: dormant coins
column11: coins that \\\"probably\\\" belong to lost wallets... ### why \\\"protecting\\\" p2pk coins is theft satoshi's coins and other early p2pk outputs have the public key directly exposed on-chain. here's the thing: there is no way to distinguish between satoshi waking up and moving his coins versus a qc attacker cracking them. maybe satoshi lost his keys. maybe he's waiting for cover of quantum computers to move them privately. maybe he deliberately left them as a bounty for cryptographic researchers: 50 bch per key
column12: canaries in the coal mine announcing when the threat arrives. we don't know
column14: we don't need to know. it's not our decision to make. the locking script is a contract encoding the funder's will. when someone funded a p2pk output
column15: they encoded exact conditions for spending: produce a valid signature from this public key. that's it. no asterisks
column16: no \\\"unless developers decide otherwise later.\\\" \\\"not your keys
column17: not your coins\\\" cuts both ways. if a quantum computer derives the key
column18: those coins become theirs: still not ours to burn or redistribute. bch survived a bigger dump than 1.7m coins during the post-fork period when btc maxis sold. someone bought those coins. someone will buy these too. ### the nuanced technical picture what the panic merchants won't tell you is that different coin types have completely different risk profiles and solutions: **p2pk (public key exposed):** ~1.7m coins including satoshi's. no ethical intervention possible: you cannot distinguish owner from attacker. treat as qc bounty or at most consider rate-limiting spends (e.g.
column19: 50/block matching original emission) to slow market impact. **p2pkh with revealed pubkey:** public key was exposed in a previous spend. owners are either active (can migrate now) or abandoned their coins. **p2pkh with unrevealed pubkey:** only the hash is on-chain. already significantly safer. stop reusing addresses. a commit-delay-reveal soft fork could protect real owners without theft: commit to your transaction hash
column21: then reveal and execute. qc attackers can't front-run because they'd need to crack the key and beat your commitment. **new coins going forward:** quantumroot vaults will become available on mainnet after may 15
column23: 128-bit quantum security using only sha256. problem solved for anyone who cares to use it. ### the bottom line bch already has the technical solutions. quantumroot is here. the may 2026 upgrade enables efficient quantum-resistant wallets. wallet developers will be integrating support. what bch doesn't need is panicked governance changes that establish precedent for coin confiscation. the \\\"cure\\\" of developer-controlled redistribution may be far worse than the \\\"disease\\\" of some old coins eventually being swept by whoever cracks them first. if someone pushes hard for freezing or burning coins while ignoring these distinctions
column24: ask yourself why. and remember the chip process: default answer is no. status quo is: qc-vulnerable coins are fair game for qc-attackers. if someone wants to change that: burden of proof is on them. watch out for another suspicious tell: presenting any solution as if it were a done deal.\"
